_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/Aortic Root Replacement Surgery/ : Aortic root replacement surgery_Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + Aortic root replacement surgery_PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Aortic valve dysfunction with aortic root aneurysm or dissection
Aortic root aneurysm with risk/presence of rupture, significant aortic regurgitation, coronary compromise, airway compression, and endocarditis
Acute aortic dissection (Type A, involving the ascending aorta) with complications such as organ malperfusion syndromes, acute aortic regurgitation, pericardial tamponade, or stroke
Connective tissue disorders (Marfan's syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome) involving the elastic aortic root
Bicuspid aortic valve with thoracic aortic dilatation
Inflammatory etiology (Giant cell arteritis, Takayasu arteritis) causing aneurysmal dilatation/aortic dissection
Cystic medial necrosis of large arteries
Acquired calcific valvular disease leading to aortic stenosis or mixed stenosis/regurgitation

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings, indication of implant/graft requirement, and planned line of management

claim.label: Post procedure stills of ECHO with report
Implant/Graft (if artificial graft is used) details - barcode/invoice

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes contain all vitals, detailed history, symptoms, signs, physical examination, indication for procedure, planned line of treatment, and advice for admission?
Echo/ Doppler report confirms the diagnosis?

cpd_claim.q: Detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details available?
Post procedure still of ECHO shows repair of the defect?
Implant or graft (if artificial graft used) invoice/barcode details available?
Discharge summary includes follow-up advice at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Echo/Doppler report does not confirm the diagnosis
Clinical presentation and imaging are not indicative of surgery
Clinical notes are incomplete or missing essential details (vitals, history, symptoms, signs, physical examination, indication, planned treatment, or advice for admission)
Post-procedure ECHO stills are missing or do not show repair of the defect
Implant or graft invoice/barcode details are missing (if artificial graft used)
Discharge summary is missing or lacks follow-up advice

clinical_key_pointers: The surgical management of aortic root pathology is complicated and challenging, based on the anatomy of the aortic root leading to complex problems needing surgical correction.

Aortic root pathology can be caused by connective tissue disorders (Marfan's, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz syndromes), bicuspid aortic valve (BAV), inflammatory etiology (Giant cell arteritis, Takayasu arteritis), cystic medial necrosis (CMN), or acquired conditions like calcific valvular disease.
Acute aortic dissection is a high-risk aortic catastrophe (occurs in 5-30/1,000,000 patients annually).
Aortic dissection is classified as acute (<14 days from symptom onset) or chronic (>14 days).
Stanford University classification for aortic dissection: Type A (involves ascending aorta) or Type B (does not involve ascending aorta).
Devastating complications of aortic dissection include organ malperfusion syndromes, acute aortic regurgitation, pericardial tamponade, and stroke.
Surgery is the gold standard for acute care of type A aortic dissection.

Most patients with aortic root pathology are asymptomatic, except those presenting with endocarditis or aortic root destruction secondary to acute type A aortic dissection (severe chest pains, asymmetric pulses, congestive heart failure).
The age range of presentation is broad (twenties to eighties), dependent on the underlying pathology.
Certain patients may exhibit characteristic stigmata of connective tissue diseases like Marfan syndrome.
Workup for aortic root replacement includes echocardiography to estimate ventricular function, aortic valve function, and possibility of valve preservation.
Coronary catheterization may be performed in selected high-risk patients.
Thin slice computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides necessary anatomical information about the aneurysm.
A careful oral examination or dental consultation is important to prevent postoperative prosthetic valve infection.
